summary: Drew DeVault argues that Google's public ban on AGPL software constitutes propaganda designed to discourage AGPL adoption across the industry. He contends that Google misrepresents the AGPL's obligations, claiming it would require releasing entire applications when in fact it only requires publishing modifications to the AGPL-licensed component itself. Google's motivation is to maintain a large pool of permissively-licensed open source software they can incorporate without giving anything back. DeVault urges developers not to be swayed by these misconceptions and to read the license themselves.
key_points: AGPL compliance is straightforward: if you modify AGPL software, publish your modifications under the same terms — that's essentially it
Google mischaracterizes AGPL as requiring release of entire applications that merely use AGPL components, when it actually only requires releasing modifications to the AGPL code itself
Google publishes its internal licensing policies publicly specifically to influence the broader developer community against AGPL — this is propaganda
The AGPL closes the 'SaaS loophole' by updating what counts as distribution, not what counts as a derivative work
Companies hoping Google will pay for their software if they avoid AGPL are deluding themselves — Google will either take it for free or build in-house
money_quotes: Google is also well-known for being the subject of cargo-culting by fad startups.

AGPL does not extend the GPL in that it makes the Internet count as a form of linking which creates a derivative work, as Google implies, but rather that it makes anyone who uses the software via the Internet entitled to its source code.
It does not update the 'what counts as a derivative work' algorithm, so to speak — it updates the 'what counts as distributing the software' algorithm.
Google wants to be able to incorporate FOSS software into their products and sell it to users without the obligation to release their derivative works.

Ask yourself: why is documentation of internal-facing decisions like what software licenses to use being published in a public place? The answer is straightforward: to influence the public. This is propaganda.

If you don't use the AGPL, they're just going to take your software and give nothing back. If you do use the AGPL, they're just going to develop a solution in-house. There's no outcome where Google pays you.
By the way, there are no more than 10 companies world-wide which are 'like Google' by any measure.
key_insight: Google's public anti-AGPL policy is not internal guidance but deliberate propaganda aimed at discouraging copyleft adoption so Google can freely exploit permissively-licensed open source without contributing back.
